draft.introduction: John 12:24–36 presents Jesus’ teaching on the necessity of death for fruitfulness, using the image of a grain of wheat falling into the earth. It marks the transition from Jesus’ public ministry to his passion.

translation.text: Truly, truly, I say to you, unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit. Whoever loves his life loses it, and whoever hates his life in this world will keep it for eternal life.
translation.original_text: ἀμὴν ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ἐὰν μὴ ὁ κόκκος τοῦ σίτου πεσὼν εἰς τὴν γῆν ἀποθάνῃ

translator_notes.note: The grain-of-wheat metaphor resonates with mystery religion imagery of dying and rising gods (Osiris, Attis, Persephone). Whether John draws on these traditions consciously or independently arrives at similar symbolism remains debated.
